questions.question: Computer/information security controls are divided into how many distinct categories?
questions.option_a: A. Two — Physical and Technical
questions.option_b: B. Three — Physical, Technical/Logical, and Administrative
questions.option_c: C. Four — Physical, Technical, Administrative, and Compensating
questions.option_d: D. Five — Preventive, Detective, Deterrent, Corrective, and Recovery
questions.explanation: Security controls are divided into three distinct categories: Physical, Technical/Logical, and Administrative.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST defines a Physical security control?
questions.option_a: A. Using encryption and access control lists to protect data on a network
questions.option_b: B. Implementing security policies and training programs for staff
questions.option_c: C. Implementation of security measures in a defined structure to deter or prevent unauthorized access to sensitive material
questions.option_d: D. Using software to monitor and log system events for irregularities
questions.explanation: Physical controls involve implementing security measures in a physical structure to deter or prevent unauthorized access to sensitive material.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a Physical security control?
questions.option_a: A. Access Control Lists (ACLs)
questions.option_b: B. File integrity auditing software
questions.option_c: C. Security awareness training
questions.option_d: D. Motion or thermal alarm systems
questions.explanation: Motion or thermal alarm systems are physical controls used to detect movement or temperature changes in protected areas.

questions.question: Network segregation and work area separation are classified under which type of security control?
questions.option_a: A. Technical/Logical controls
questions.option_b: B. Administrative controls
questions.option_c: C. Physical controls
questions.option_d: D. Compensating controls
questions.explanation: Network segregation and work area separation are physical controls because they restrict access through physical or structural design.

questions.question: Technical/Logical controls use technology to control access and usage of sensitive data. Which of the following is a Technical control?
questions.option_a: A. Security guards at building entrances
questions.option_b: B. Separation of duties policies
questions.option_c: C. Personnel recruitment strategies
questions.option_d: D. Encryption and smart cards
questions.explanation: Encryption and smart cards are technical controls because they use technology to control access to sensitive information.

questions.question: Access Control Lists (ACLs) and file integrity auditing software belong to which category of security control?
questions.option_a: A. Physical controls
questions.option_b: B. Administrative controls
questions.option_c: C. Compensating controls
questions.option_d: D. Technical/Logical controls
questions.explanation: ACLs and file integrity auditing software are technical or logical controls because they rely on software and system mechanisms.

questions.question: Administrative controls are best described as:
questions.option_a: A. Technology-based mechanisms that restrict access to data on a network
questions.option_b: B. Physical barriers installed to prevent unauthorized entry to secure areas
questions.option_c: C. Controls that define the human factors of security and determine access at all personnel levels
questions.option_d: D. Backup systems deployed after a security violation occurs
questions.explanation: Administrative controls define human aspects of security such as policies, procedures, and personnel management.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an Administrative security control?
questions.option_a: A. Surveillance cameras
questions.option_b: B. Encryption algorithms
questions.option_c: C. Network authentication
questions.option_d: D. Disaster preparedness and recovery plans
questions.explanation: Disaster preparedness and recovery plans are administrative controls because they involve organizational policies and planning.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly lists all the functional categories of security controls?
questions.option_a: A. Physical, Technical, Administrative, and Compensating
questions.option_b: B. Preventive, Detective, Corrective, and Recovery
questions.option_c: C. Preventive, Detective, Deterrent, Corrective, Recovery, and Compensating
questions.option_d: D. Proactive, Reactive, Restorative, and Alternative
questions.explanation: Functional security control categories include Preventive, Detective, Deterrent, Corrective, Recovery, and Compensating.

questions.question: Preventive controls are best described as:
questions.option_a: A. Controls designed to detect and report security violations after they occur
questions.option_b: B. Controls that fix the situation after a security violation has happened
questions.option_c: C. Proactive controls designed to discourage errors or irregularities from occurring
questions.option_d: D. Alternative controls used when primary controls cannot be used
questions.explanation: Preventive controls are proactive measures that stop errors or security incidents before they occur.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a Preventive control?
questions.option_a: A. Log reviews
questions.option_b: B. Monitoring systems
questions.option_c: C. Disaster recovery mechanisms
questions.option_d: D. Separation of duties
questions.explanation: Separation of duties prevents fraud or errors by ensuring no single person controls all parts of a critical process.

questions.question: Detective controls are specifically designed to:
questions.option_a: A. Stop security violations before they happen
questions.option_b: B. Discourage attackers from attempting an intrusion
questions.option_c: C. Find errors or irregularities after they have occurred
questions.option_d: D. Restore systems to their normal state after an attack
questions.explanation: Detective controls identify and report errors or irregularities after they occur.

questions.question: Security reviews, audits, and log reviews are examples of which type of control?
questions.option_a: A. Preventive controls
questions.option_b: B. Corrective controls
questions.option_c: C. Compensating controls
questions.option_d: D. Detective controls
questions.explanation: Audits and log reviews detect irregularities and security incidents after they occur.

questions.question: Deterrent controls are intended to:
questions.option_a: A. Automatically block unauthorized access attempts using technology
questions.option_b: B. Restore normal operations after a serious security incident
questions.option_c: C. Discourage potential attackers by signalling that attacks will be detected and defended
questions.option_d: D. Identify vulnerabilities in systems before attackers do
questions.explanation: Deterrent controls discourage attackers by signaling strong monitoring and security practices.

questions.question: A security team updates firewall rules immediately after detecting an attacker's IP address. This is an example of which control?
questions.option_a: A. Preventive control
questions.option_b: B. Detective control
questions.option_c: C. Deterrent control
questions.option_d: D. Corrective control
questions.explanation: Corrective controls address and fix issues after a security incident occurs.

questions.question: Recovery controls differ from Corrective controls in that they:
questions.option_a: A. Focus on preventing security violations before they happen
questions.option_b: B. Are applied in more serious situations to restore information and processing resources
questions.option_c: C. Are used only when all other controls have been bypassed
questions.option_d: D. Focus on deterring attackers rather than fixing damage
questions.explanation: Recovery controls restore systems and data after serious security incidents.

questions.question: Compensating controls are best described as:
questions.option_a: A. Controls that prevent unauthorized physical access to a building
questions.option_b: B. Controls specifically designed to detect irregularities in real-time
questions.option_c: C. Alternative arrangements for other controls when original controls have failed or cannot be used
questions.option_d: D. Controls that punish employees who breach security policies
questions.explanation: Compensating controls provide alternative protections when primary controls cannot be implemented.

questions.question: Information Risk Management (IRM) is defined as:
questions.option_a: A. The elimination of all risks within an organisation's IT infrastructure
questions.option_b: B. The process of identifying and assessing risk, reducing it to an acceptable level, and implementing mechanisms to maintain that level
questions.option_c: C. A regulatory framework that mandates specific security controls for all industries
questions.option_d: D. The transfer of all risk responsibilities to a third-party provider
questions.explanation: IRM involves identifying risks, reducing them to acceptable levels, and maintaining those levels through controls.

questions.question: In risk management terminology, a Vulnerability is defined as:
questions.option_a: A. The likelihood of a threat agent exploiting a weakness
questions.option_b: B. The potential financial loss resulting from a security incident
questions.option_c: C. A system, network, or device weakness
questions.option_d: D. The entity that identifies and exploits a weakness to attack a victim
questions.explanation: A vulnerability is a weakness in a system, network, or device that can be exploited.

questions.question: What is a Threat Agent in the context of risk management?
questions.option_a: A. A software tool that scans for vulnerabilities in systems
questions.option_b: B. The potential danger posed by a weakness in a system or device
questions.option_c: C. A countermeasure put in place to mitigate potential risk
questions.option_d: D. The entity that identifies a vulnerability and uses it to attack the victim
questions.explanation: A threat agent is the entity that exploits vulnerabilities to carry out attacks.

questions.question: An organisation decides to purchase cyber insurance to shift the financial responsibility of a risk to a third party. Which risk management strategy is this?
questions.option_a: A. Accept
questions.option_b: B. Mitigate
questions.option_c: C. Avoid
questions.option_d: D. Transfer
questions.explanation: Risk transfer shifts financial responsibility to another party such as an insurance provider.

questions.question: A company decides to shut down an outdated system that poses a high security risk rather than investing in securing it. Which risk management approach is this?
questions.option_a: A. Transfer
questions.option_b: B. Accept
questions.option_c: C. Mitigate
questions.option_d: D. Avoid
questions.explanation: Risk avoidance eliminates exposure to the risk by removing the vulnerable asset or activity.

questions.question: In Quantitative Risk Analysis, what does SLE (Single Loss Expectancy) represent?
questions.option_a: A. The annual cost of implementing a security safeguard
questions.option_b: B. The frequency with which a threat is expected to occur within a year
questions.option_c: C. The percentage of asset value that would be lost in a single incident
questions.option_d: D. The financial loss expected from a single occurrence of a threat (Asset Value × Exposure Factor)
questions.explanation: SLE equals Asset Value multiplied by Exposure Factor and represents the loss from one incident.

questions.question: A server is valued at Rs. 200,000. A flood is estimated to cause 40% damage and is expected to occur once every 5 years (ARO = 0.2). What is the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)?
The Safeguard Cost/Benefit formula in Quantitative Risk Analysis is:
questions.option_a: A. ALE before Safeguard + ALE after Safeguard + Annual Cost of Safeguard
questions.option_b: B. ALE before Safeguard − ALE after Safeguard − Annual Cost of Safeguard
questions.option_c: C. SLE × ARO − Annual Cost of Safeguard
questions.option_d: D. Asset Value × Exposure Factor − Annual Cost of Safeguard
questions.explanation: Safeguard Cost/Benefit equals ALE before safeguard minus ALE after safeguard minus the annual cost of the safeguard.
